title_en: The Paraclete in the Gospel of John
content_en:
The Gospel of John contains a series of passages in which Jesus promises his disciples the coming of a figure described in Greek as the 'Paraclete' — a word that the standard English translations render as 'Comforter,' 'Helper,' 'Counselor,' or 'Advocate.' These passages, found in John chapters 14, 15, and 16, have generated enormous theological interest across the centuries, with Christian interpreters universally identifying the Paraclete with the Holy Spirit and Muslim scholars arguing that the original text referred not to a spirit but to the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him).

The Muslim argument regarding the Paraclete begins with a textual observation. The Greek word Paraclete (parakletos) means 'one who is called alongside to help.' However, there exists another Greek word — periklutos — that means 'the praised one,' 'the celebrated one,' or 'the glorious one.' Muslim scholars, including Deedat, argue that the original word in the text of the Gospel was periklutos, which is precisely the Greek translation of the Arabic name 'Ahmad' — one of the Prophet Muhammad's names, meaning 'the most praised.' The Quran itself records Jesus saying: 'And [mention] when Jesus, the son of Mary, said: O children of Israel, indeed I am the messenger of Allah to you confirming what came before me of the Torah and bringing good tidings of a messenger to come after me whose name is Ahmad' (61:6).

Deedat notes that the characteristics attributed to the Paraclete in John's Gospel align far more naturally with Muhammad than with the Holy Spirit as understood in Christian theology. Jesus says of the Paraclete: 'He will not speak on his own authority, but whatever he hears he will speak' (John 16:13). This description of a figure who transmits what he receives rather than speaking independently corresponds precisely to the Islamic description of prophetic revelation — the Prophet Muhammad received the Quran from Jibril and conveyed it without personal addition or alteration. The Holy Spirit, as the third person of the Trinity in Christian theology, is co-equal and co-eternal with the Father and the Son, and the notion that the Spirit 'speaks only what he hears' sits awkwardly with this theological status.

Further, Jesus says: 'He will glorify me' (John 16:14). The Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) consistently honored and glorified Jesus as a great prophet and messenger of Allah, affirming his miraculous birth, his extraordinary character, and his prophetic mission. Islam gives Jesus a far more exalted position than most secular historians of first-century Palestine, and the Muslim Ummah's deep respect for Jesus — combined with its rejection of the theological errors that the Quran associates with certain of his followers — fulfills this promise in a meaningful sense.

Jesus also says: 'It is for your good that I am going away. Unless I go away, the Paraclete will not come to you' (John 16:7). This statement implies that the coming of the Paraclete is contingent upon the physical departure of Jesus — something that would make little sense if the Paraclete were the Holy Spirit that had been present and active since the creation and that the Gospels themselves describe as active during Jesus's own ministry (descending upon him at his baptism, leading him into the wilderness, etc.). However, it makes perfect sense if the Paraclete is a human prophet who would come after Jesus's departure from the world.

The textual and theological arguments regarding the Paraclete remain a subject of ongoing scholarly debate, and Christian scholars have developed detailed responses to the Muslim interpretation. What Deedat's analysis establishes, at minimum, is that these Gospel passages raise genuine questions about the identity of the promised figure that cannot be dismissed without careful engagement.
content_id:
Injil Yohanes mengandung serangkaian bagian di mana Isa menjanjikan kepada murid-muridnya kedatangan seorang tokoh yang digambarkan dalam bahasa Yunani sebagai 'Parakletos' — sebuah kata yang oleh terjemahan-terjemahan bahasa Inggris standar diterjemahkan sebagai 'Penghibur,' 'Penolong,' 'Penasihat,' atau 'Pembela.' Bagian-bagian ini, yang ditemukan dalam pasal-pasal 14, 15, dan 16 Injil Yohanes, telah menghasilkan minat teologis yang luar biasa selama berabad-abad, dengan para penafsir Kristen secara universal mengidentifikasi Parakletos dengan Roh Kudus dan para ulama Muslim berargumen bahwa teks aslinya merujuk bukan kepada roh melainkan kepada Nabi Muhammad ﷺ.

Argumen Muslim mengenai Parakletos dimulai dengan pengamatan tekstual. Kata Yunani Parakletos (parakletos) berarti 'seseorang yang dipanggil untuk membantu.' Namun, ada kata Yunani lain — periklutos — yang berarti 'yang dipuji,' 'yang dirayakan,' atau 'yang mulia.' Para ulama Muslim, termasuk Deedat, berargumen bahwa kata asli dalam teks Injil adalah periklutos, yang merupakan terjemahan Yunani yang tepat dari nama Arab 'Ahmad' — salah satu nama Nabi Muhammad, yang berarti 'yang paling dipuji.' Al-Quran sendiri mencatat Isa berkata: 'Dan [ingatlah] ketika Isa putra Maryam berkata: Hai Bani Israel, sesungguhnya aku adalah utusan Allah kepadamu, membenarkan kitab sebelumku, yaitu Taurat, dan memberi khabar gembira dengan seorang Rasul yang akan datang sesudahku, yang namanya Ahmad' (61:6).

Deedat mencatat bahwa karakteristik-karakteristik yang dikaitkan dengan Parakletos dalam Injil Yohanes jauh lebih alami selaras dengan Muhammad ﷺ daripada dengan Roh Kudus sebagaimana dipahami dalam teologi Kristen. Isa berkata tentang Parakletos: 'Ia tidak akan berkata-kata dari diri-Nya sendiri, tetapi segala sesuatu yang didengar-Nya itulah yang akan dikatakan-Nya' (Yohanes 16:13). Deskripsi tentang seorang tokoh yang meneruskan apa yang diterimanya daripada berbicara secara independen ini bersesuaian persis dengan deskripsi Islam tentang wahyu kenabian — Nabi Muhammad ﷺ menerima Al-Quran dari Jibril dan menyampaikannya tanpa penambahan pribadi atau perubahan. Roh Kudus, sebagai orang ketiga dari Trinitas dalam teologi Kristen, setara dan seabadi dengan Bapa dan Putra, dan gagasan bahwa Roh 'hanya berbicara apa yang didengarnya' terasa janggal dengan status teologis ini.

Selanjutnya, Isa berkata: 'Ia akan memuliakan Aku' (Yohanes 16:14). Nabi Muhammad ﷺ secara konsisten menghormati dan memuliakan Isa sebagai nabi besar dan utusan Allah, menegaskan kelahiran ajaibnya, karakternya yang luar biasa, dan misi kenabiannya. Islam memberikan Isa posisi yang jauh lebih mulia daripada sebagian besar sejarawan sekuler Palestina abad pertama, dan penghormatan mendalam umat Muslim kepada Isa — digabungkan dengan penolakannya terhadap kesalahan-kesalahan teologis yang Al-Quran kaitkan dengan sebagian pengikutnya — memenuhi janji ini dalam pengertian yang bermakna.

Isa juga berkata: 'Adalah lebih berguna bagi kamu, jika Aku pergi. Sebab jikalau Aku tidak pergi, Penghibur itu tidak akan datang kepadamu' (Yohanes 16:7). Pernyataan ini menyiratkan bahwa kedatangan Parakletos bergantung pada kepergian fisik Isa — sesuatu yang tidak masuk akal jika Parakletos adalah Roh Kudus yang telah hadir dan aktif sejak penciptaan dan yang Injil-Injil itu sendiri gambarkan sebagai aktif selama pelayanan Isa sendiri (turun ke atasnya pada pembaptisannya, memimpinnya ke padang gurun, dll.). Namun, hal itu sangat masuk akal jika Parakletos adalah seorang nabi manusia yang akan datang setelah kepergian Isa dari dunia.

Argumen-argumen tekstual dan teologis mengenai Parakletos tetap menjadi subjek debat akademis yang berkelanjutan, dan para ulama Kristen telah mengembangkan tanggapan-tanggapan terperinci atas interpretasi Muslim. Yang ditetapkan oleh analisis Deedat, setidaknya, adalah bahwa bagian-bagian Injil ini mengangkat pertanyaan-pertanyaan yang sah tentang identitas tokoh yang dijanjikan yang tidak dapat diabaikan tanpa keterlibatan yang cermat.
content_ar: The Gospel of John contains a series of passages in which عيسى ﷺ promises his disciples the coming of a figure described in Greek as the 'Paraclete' — a word that the standard English translations render as 'Comforter,' 'Helper,' 'Counselor,' or 'Advocate.' These passages, found in John الأبواب 14, 15, and 16, have generated enormous theological interest across the centuries, with Christian interpreters universally identifying the Paraclete with the Holy الروح and مسلم العلماء arguing that the original text referred not to a الروح but to النبي ﷺ محمد ﷺ (ﷺ). The مسلم argument فيما يخص the Paraclete begins with a textual observation. The Greek word Paraclete (parakletos) means 'one who is called alongside to help.' غير أن, there exists another Greek word — periklutos — that means 'the praised one,' 'the celebrated one,' or 'the glorious one.' مسلم العلماء, بما في ذلك Deedat, argue that the original word in the text of the Gospel was periklutos, which is precisely the Greek translation of the Arabic name 'أحمد' — one of النبي ﷺ محمد ﷺ's names, meaning 'the most praised.' The القرآن الكريم itself records عيسى ﷺ saying: 'And [mention] عندما عيسى ﷺ, the son of Mary, said: O الأولاد of Israel, indeed I am الرسول ﷺ of الله to you confirming what came قبل me of the Torah and bringing الخير tidings of a messenger to come بعد me whose name is أحمد' (61:6). Deedat notes that the characteristics attributed to the Paraclete in John's Gospel align far more naturally with محمد ﷺ than with the Holy الروح as understood in Christian theology. عيسى ﷺ says of the Paraclete: 'He will not speak on his own authority, but whatever he hears he will speak' (John 16:13). This description of a figure who transmits what he receives rather than speaking independently corresponds precisely to the Islamic description of prophetic الوحي — النبي ﷺ محمد ﷺ received the القرآن الكريم from جبريل ﷺ and conveyed it without personal addition or alteration. The Holy الروح, as the ثالثاً person of the Trinity in Christian theology, is co-equal and co-eternal with the الأب and the Son, and the notion that the الروح 'speaks only what he hears' sits awkwardly with this theological status. Further, عيسى ﷺ says: 'He will glorify me' (John 16:14). النبي ﷺ محمد ﷺ (ﷺ) consistently honored and glorified عيسى ﷺ as a great النبي and messenger of الله, affirming his miraculous birth, his extraordinary الأخلاق, and his prophetic mission. الإسلام gives عيسى ﷺ a far more exalted position than most secular historians of أولاً-century Palestine, and the مسلم الأمة's deep الاحترام for عيسى ﷺ — combined with its rejection of the theological errors that the القرآن الكريم associates with certain of his followers — fulfills this promise in a meaningful sense. عيسى ﷺ أيضاً says: 'It is for your الخير that I am going away. إلا إذا I go away, the Paraclete will not come to you' (John 16:7).
